CLARITY.justification: The paper has a recognizable section structure (e.g. sections 3–5) but the logical flow is disrupted—the main claim (an automated short-answer scoring system) only appears late and background material is repeated (multiple duplicate paragraphs about Jess/NYU). Notation and variable names in figures (e.g. sa_jpkwrel_..., SA_sim) are unexplained and inconsistent, and tables/figures are poorly presented (Table 2/3 are embedded with broken formatting and Figure 4 contents are cluttered and hard to read). The writing contains frequent grammatical awkwardness and errors (e.g. “that that,” run-on passages, and duplicated sentences), making important parts of the methods and results difficult to follow.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies standard, well-known techniques (random forests for multi-class prediction (Sec. 4.1), cosine semantic similarity, and surface keyword collation) to Japanese short-answer scoring rather than proposing a new algorithmic idea. It explicitly cites prior automated scoring systems (ETS c-rater, NYU/ETS, and the authors' own Jess) and frames the work as an applied prototype with features like mandatory-keyword handling and a rejected case-grammar probe (Sec. 4.3). Overall this is an incremental, application-driven combination of existing methods with limited methodological novelty.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper cites prior systems (Shermis & Burstein 2013; NYU/ETS Vigilante 1999; Leacock & Chodorow 2003; Pulman & Sukkarieh 2005; Jess Ishioka & Kameda 2006) and discusses RFs (Breiman 2001) but presents only comparisons of its automated scores vs human ratings on its eight-item trial ("We compared human scores with the automated scores for a case... 8 categories"). It does not provide empirical baselines against prior automated systems (e.g., c-rater, Jess) or alternative algorithms on the same data, nor fair head-to-head metrics or ablations; while limitations are acknowledged, key comparative experiments are missing. Therefore comparisons are inadequate and key baselines are not evaluated.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important, applied problem—automated short-answer scoring for large-scale Japanese exams—and presents a working prototype with empirical results (differences within one point for 70–95% of items when high semantic judgement was not required) and features tailored to practical deployment (e.g., mandatory-keyword handling and a web system). However, the approach primarily applies established techniques (Random Forests, cosine semantic similarity) and reports clear limitations on hard semantic cases (World History B2 #3) and on skewed score distributions, so it is unlikely to fundamentally change research directions. It is nonetheless useful and likely to influence practitioners and localized deployments (e.g., Japanese national testing) and to motivate follow-up work on robust semantic entailment and better training-data collection.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses an NLP/ML application (automated short-answer scoring, textual entailment and semantic similarity) so the topic is in scope, but it is primarily an applied system report using Random Forests and hand-crafted features on a very small evaluation (8 items, sample sizes 70–120) with limited evaluation (no held-out splits/baselines, omitted confusion matrices) and no engagement with modern representation/entailment methods. That makes it more appropriate for an applied/education venue or workshop than a top-tier NLP/ML conference, as it lacks the methodological novelty, dataset scale, and rigorous evaluation standards expected by that community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents experiments on 8 short-answer items with per-item results (Table 3: 0.48–0.95 within-one-point rates, sample sizes 70–120) and some variable-importance plots, and it discusses concrete failure cases (World History B2 #3, JpnHistory B1 #2) and broader limitations (insufficient labeled data, many zero scores). However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (no systematic feature-removal tests), omits confusion matrices and detailed error analyses, and even admits possible overfitting when comparing training data to final RF estimates. Related work is cited (c-rater, Jess, ETS/NYU) though a bit repetitive, but reproducibility is weak because feature definitions, preprocessing, exact predictors, hyperparameters, and data/code are not provided.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main empirical claim—that a Random Forest–based scorer attains within-one-point agreement of ~71–95% on six of eight short-answer items—is supported by Table 3 and reported per-item rates, but the evaluation is limited (only 8 items, sample sizes 70–120) and important details are omitted (confusion matrices, statistical tests, held-out/replication results). Methodological weaknesses include severe class imbalance (noted for Jpn History B1 #2), no comparison baselines (e.g. SVM or existing c-rater), and reliance on OOB error with no further validation; the statement that RFs “do not need cross validation” is misleading. Overall the conclusions about a viable first-step system are plausibly supported, but several experimental and inferential gaps leave some claims under-supported.
